coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots unraveling: Green synthesis, characterization, radiolabeling with 99mTc, in vivo behavior and mutagenicity
coredata.dc:creator: de Menezes, Frederico Duarte
dos Reis, Sara Rhaissa Rezende
Pinto, Suyene Rocha
Portilho, Filipe Leal
do Vale Chaves e Mello, Francisco
Helal-Neto, Edward
da Silva de Barros, Aline Oliveira
Alencar, Luciana Magalhães Rebêlo
de Menezes, Alan Silva
dos Santos, Clenilton Costa
Saraiva-Souza, Aldilene
Perini, Jamila Alessandra
Machado, Daniel Escorsim
Felzenswalb, Israel
Araujo-Lima, Carlos Fernando
Sukhanova, Alyona
Nabiev, Igor
Santos-Oliveira, Ralph
coredata.dc:description: Graphene is one of the crystalline forms of carbon, along with diamond, graphite, carbon nanotubes, and fullerenes, and is considered as a revolutionary and innovating product. The use of a graphene-based nanolabels is one of the latest and most prominent application of graphene, especially in the field of diagnosis and, recently, in loco radiotherapy when coupled with radioisotopes. However, its biological behavior and mutagenicity in different cell or animal models, as well as the in vivo functional activities, are still unrevealed. In this study we have developed by a green route of synthesizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and characterized them. We have also developed a methodology for direct radiolabeling of GQDs with radioisotopes.Finally; we have evaluated in vivo biological behavior of GQDs using two different mice models and tested in vitro mutagenicity of GQDs. The results have shown that GQDs were formed with a size range of 160-280 nm, which was confirmed by DRX and Raman spectroscopy analysis, corroborating that the green synthesis is an alternative, environmentally friendly way to produce graphene. The radiolabeling test has shown that stable radiolabeled GQDs can be produced with a high yield (>90%). The in vivo test has demonstrated a ubiquitous behavior when administered to healthy animals, with a high uptake by liver (>26%) and small intestine (>25%). Otherwise, in an inflammation/VEGF hyperexpression animal model (endometriosis), a very peculiar behavior of GQDs was observed, with a high uptake by kidneys (over 85%). The mutagenicity test has demonstrated A:T to G:C substitutions suggesting that GQDs exhibits mutagenic activity.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Size determination by dynamic light scattering
Quality control of the labeled GQDs with 99mTc
Size determination by dynamic light scattering
Radiochemical purity of the GQDs nanoparticles

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Dynamic light scattering analysis of the ultra-high-diluted sample (1:400.000) of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The mean size was of 167.3 nm.

Raman Analysis showing the spectrum of GO, where the in-phase vibration (G band) of GO is ~1600 cm-1 and the disorder band (D band) of GO is at ~1360 cm. Is possible to observe the intense bands D and G at 1356 and 1582 cm-1, respectively, with the intensity ratio between ID/IG of the D and G peak relatively high (~ 1.2), corroborating the purity of the graphene. Also, is not observed a peak at ~ 2690 cm-1, which indicates the formation of the 2D structure, corroborating in this case, the formation of the Graphene Quantum Dots.

Atomic force microscopic analysis of the graphene quantum dotsshowing their size distribution (160-280 nm). A good homogeneity and the height of the nanoparticles showing a low roughness and the presence of coverslips are seen.
The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis of sample showing the formation of graphene peak at 2θ = 10, as also the presence of citrate.
The optical properties of the graphene quantum dots. Panel A shows absorption spectrum of GQDs with a maximum at 265 nm and the Panel B shows fluorescence spectrum with the maximum around 530 nm.
Biodistribution of 99mTc-GQDin healthy animals (Balb/c mice) 2 h after retro-orbital injection.

(A, B) Morphological characteristics of the experimental mice endometriosis model showing the eutopic endometrium 15 days after induction. Histologically, the endometriotic tissues were similar to the eutopic endometrium. (C) VEGF immunodistribution of eutopic endometrium. (D) Morphometric analysis confirmed the increase in the VEGF level in the endometriotic lesions compared with the eutopic endometrium. (E) The expression of mRNA encoding VEGF also showed that the levels of VEGF were significantly higher in endometriosis than in the eutopic endometrium.

Biodistribution of 99mTc-GQDs in inducted animals with endometriosis 2 h after the injection, mimicking a high expression of VEGF and reflecting the behavior of GQDs in the environment. In this figure, lesion means the endometriotic region, the highest zone of expression of VEGF.

Revertant colonies of the TA98, TA100, TA102, and TA104 strains of theSalmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium His
+ in the Ames test after incubation with graphene quantum dots. *, Differencefrom the negative control; ¢,cytotoxic;one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey's post hoc test (p < 0.05). Positive controls (P.C.) were 4-nitroquinoline-N-oxide (1.0 μg/plate) for TA98; sodium azide(1.0 μg/plate) for TA100; mytomicin-C (0.5 μg/plate) for TA102; and methyl methanesulphonate (250 μg/plate) for TA104.
PXRD analysis of GQDs showing the presence of citrate in the graphene oxide sample due the fabrication route used.
Radiochemical purity (%) of 99mTc GQDs in 2 and 4 h.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots unraveling: Green synthesis, characterization, radiolabeling with 99mTc, in vivo behavior and mutagenicity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene is one of the crystalline forms of carbon, along with diamond, graphite, carbon nanotubes, and fullerenes, and is considered as a revolutionary and innovating product. The use of a graphene-based nanolabels is one of the latest and most prominent application of graphene, especially in the field of diagnosis and, recently, in loco radiotherapy when coupled with radioisotopes. However, its biological behavior and mutagenicity in different cell or animal models, as well as the in vivo functional activities, are still unrevealed. In this study we have developed by a green route of synthesizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and characterized them. We have also developed a methodology for direct radiolabeling of GQDs with radioisotopes.Finally; we have evaluated in vivo biological behavior of GQDs using two different mice models and tested in vitro mutagenicity of GQDs. The results have shown that GQDs were formed with a size range of 160-280 nm, which was confirmed by DRX and Raman spectroscopy analysis, corroborating that the green synthesis is an alternative, environmentally friendly way to produce graphene. The radiolabeling test has shown that stable radiolabeled GQDs can be produced with a high yield (>90%). The in vivo test has demonstrated a ubiquitous behavior when administered to healthy animals, with a high uptake by liver (>26%) and small intestine (>25%). Otherwise, in an inflammation/VEGF hyperexpression animal model (endometriosis), a very peculiar behavior of GQDs was observed, with a high uptake by kidneys (over 85%). The mutagenicity test has demonstrated A:T to G:C substitutions suggesting that GQDs exhibits mutagenic activity.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the use of graphene based nanomaterials has increased worldwide. This new class of nanomaterials includes: graphene oxide (GO), nanographene oxide (NGO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and nano-reduced graphene oxide (nano-rGO) [1–3]. Among all the family of graphene nanomaterials, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is the most prominent one. They are nano-sized monolayers of graphene sheets with two-dimensional hexagonal lattice structure, which exhibits quantum confinement. The quantum confinement property is due the fact that at nanolevel the Broglie wavelength of an electron is comparable with the nanosystem itself. Since the quantum confinement is dependent on the size of the system, the GQDs may generate different photoluminescence (PL) spectra as a function of the size that is variable and can be tailored. Therefore, GQDs also have other properties that includes: i) size-tunable emission; ii) chemical and optical stability, iii) biocompatibility, iv) low toxicity, v) fluorescence emission, vi) NIR light absorption, vii) photothermal properties and viii) typical Raman bands. Also, GQDs may be functionalized, especially due to its surface-volume ration and presence of sp2 network that allows π–π stacking interactions. The whole of these properties can be used to enhance or complement GQDs properties to achieve synergic theranostic effects [4–8]. Thus, considering all the properties and features of GQDs, two major areas of interest where GQDs can be used are: bioimaging and drug delivery. The first one, use the luminescence to investigate and visualize biological matter, and the second one, use the surface-volume ratio and the ease to decorating feature. In both cases, a well understanding of the biological behavior is required in order to reach the best result.

Is important to notice that several studies discussing the biological behavior of GQDs were performed, many of them brought controversial data. A stinging clause among researchers leading with nanoparticles, involves the ability of nanoparticles to cross biological barriers such as blood-brain, placental and the cellular barriers in order to produce the desired effect. Is well known that nanoparticles may reach the target by two distinct mechanisms: transcellular and paracellular pathways. In the first case, nanoparticles cross the epithelial cell barrier via transcytosis. In the second, the nanoparticles pass through the tight junctions (TJs) [11,12]. In the case of GQDs, studies have reported that they are able to cross the cell membrane by a lipid raft-mediated transcytosis [13]. Therefore, despite the claim of low toxicity, some studies have reported that GQDs may induce oxidative stress in DNA level [9,10,14,15]. Although many studies regarding the GQDs biological behavior have been reported most of them were performed in vitro (cells), which differs drastically from the in vivo (animals). In this direction we developed, characterized, and evaluated in vivo the biological behavior of GQDs in two different models: healthy mice) and endometriotic rats (mimicking the disorganized tissue composition related to cancer proliferation – angiogenesis model). Also, we have performed the mutagenicity assay in order to better understand the oxidative stress mechanism involved in the exposition to QGDs.

The procedure used to produce GQDs dispersions was based on an earlier published study [16]. Briefly, a graphite rod and a platinum wire were used as an anode and a cathode, respectively. The electrolyte used was prepared by mixing 63.5 mL of 0.2 M citric acid with 36.5 mL of 0.2 M sodium citrate to obtain 100 mL of electrolyte. The electrochemical process was conducted at a constant current of 190 mA for 24 h using an ICEL PS-1500 adjustable power supply. After the oxidation electrolysis, the dispersion obtained was filtered to remove large particles. The filtered suspension was dried at 60 °C to reduce the volume of the dispersion to 10 mL. Finally, 50 mL of ethanol was added to the dispersion, and the upper phase corresponding to purified GQDs was collected. The purified GQDs were dried at 60 °C until use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size determination by dynamic light scattering
ce:section.ce:para: The size distribution, mean size, and polydispersity index (PDI) of the GQDs were determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a Zetasizer Nano ZS equipment (Malvern Instruments, UK). The measurements were performed in triplicate at 25 °C and a laser incidence angle relative to the sample of173° using a 12-mm2 quartz cuvette. The mean ± standard deviation (SD) was assessed. In order to evaluate the problems related to graphene DLS analysis, an ultra-diluted solution (1:400.000) in Milli-Q water was used.

The Raman spectra were obtained using a Horiba-Jobin-Yvon triple spectrometer (model T64000). The instrument was equipped with a confocal attachment and a liquid-N2-cooled charge coupled device (CCD) detector. For excitation, a green line (532.0 nm) from a solid-state laser (LAS-532-100-HREV) operating at 14 mW was employed. The light was focused on the sample using an Olympus microscope with an MPLN 100× objective lens. The spot of the scattered light was limited by the confocal attachment. The spectra were acquired in two ways: (i) at different points on the sample surface and (ii) by varying the position along the vertical direction centered on a particle.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) experiments were performed with a Multimode 8 (Bruker, Santa Barbara) using the Nanoscope Analysis 1.50software (Bruker) in the tapping mode (intermittent contact) for acquisition of topographic and phase images in order to determine the diameter, height, and phase composition of the particles. Experiments were performed with a cantilever spring constant of 0.24 N/m and a nominal tip radius of 2 nm. The particle solutions were diluted to a concentration of approximately 109to 1010particles per 1 cm3. The solutions were dropped in fresh cleaved mica and left to dry in a vacuum chamber protected from contamination. The experiment was performed with a scan resolution of 256 × 256 lines and a frequency of 0.5 Hz.

The optical properties of the purified GQD dispersion were estimated using absorbance and fluorescence spectroscopy by means of a Cary 60 UV–Vis (Agilent) and an Ocean Optics HR2000 spectrophotometers, respectively. For the fluorescence analysis, the sample was excited with a LED at an emission wavelength of395 nm.

The characteristics of GQDs were estimated by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD). PXRD measurements were done using a Bruker AXS D8 Advance diffractometer equipped with a CuKα radiation source and operating at 40 mA,/40 kV. PXRD measurements were performed in high angle (2θ = 15–68°) and low angle (2θ = 1.3–8.3°) ranges.

The labeling process was done using 150 μg of GQDs incubated in 100 μL of stannous chloride (SnCl2) solutions (80 μL/mL) (Sigma-Aldrich) for 20 min at room temperature. Then, this solution was incubated with 100 μCi (approximately 300 μL) of technetium-99 m for another 10 min in order to label the GQD structures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quality control of the labeled GQDs with 99mTc
ce:section.ce:para: In order to confirm the efficiency of the labeling process, Radio Thin Layer Chromatography (RTLC) was done using Whatman paper no.1 and 2 μL of the 99mTc-GQDsin acetone (Sigma-Aldrich) as a mobile phase at time points of 2 and 4 h. The radioactivity of the strips was verified in a γ-counter (Perkin Elmer Wizard® 2470, Shelton, CT, USA). The RTLC was performed in triplicate for each time point.

The GQDs were analyzed under two distinct conditions. The first one we evaluated the biological behavior of GQDs in healthy Balb/C mice. The second one we evaluated the biological behavior of the GQDs in rats inducted with endometriosis. Is important to notice that we have used endometriotic rats in order to evaluate the influence of angiogenesis in GQDs nanoparticles tissue deposition. The endometriosis model is a general method that mimics the basal inflammation and the formation of new vessels in tumor without the interference of any specific oncogenesis [17] Healthy Animals.

Animals (male Balb/c mice, n = 6) were treated in accordance with the protocols no. 182/2017 approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Internal Review Board of the Institute of Energy and Nuclear Research. The mice received water and food ad libitum and were used after reaching maturity at 6 weeks of age.

The animals (n = 6, female Wistar rats) were treated in accordance with protocols approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Internal Review Board of the Federal University of Rio de Janeiro (IBCCF-009/2008). The endometriosis model was established as previously described [18]. In brief, the abdominal cavities of six female Wistar rats were opened through a 3-cm midline incision to expose the uterus. One uterine horn was ligated at both the uterotubal junction and the cervical end and was removed. The segment was placed in phosphate-buffered saline at 37 °C, split longitudinally, and 5 × 5 mm pieces were sectioned. These explants were then anchored onto the peritoneum on the right side of the ventral abdominal wall by non-absorbable polypropylene sutures (Prolene 6–0; Ethicon, Piscataway, NJ, USA). The abdomen was closed, and the animals were allowed to recover from anesthesia. A total of 6 mice of this group were used in this study.

In order to confirm the endometriosis formation both histology and immunohistochemistry of lesions were performed. In brief, formalin-fixed tissues were paraffin-embedded and cut into 4-μm-thick sections. Harris' hematoxylin and eosin staining was performed, and the tissues were examined microscopically for the presence of histological hallmarks of endometriosis. The other paraffin-embedded tissue sections were placed on poly-l-lysine-treated slides and kept at room temperature. After dewaxing, the sections were treated with a solution of 3% H2O2 in 0.01 mol/L phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), pH 7.5, to inhibit endogenous peroxidase activity. The slides were then immersed in 0.01 M citrate buffer (pH 6.0) and heated in a microwave oven for 15 min to retrieve masked antigens. To reduce nonspecific antibody binding, the sections were then incubated with PBS containing 4% Molico®milk powder and 5% bovine serum albumin for 30 min. The sections were incubated with polyclonal anti-VEGF antibodies (Abcam, ab46154) ata dilution of 1:200. The slides were incubated overnight and then were washed three times in PBS and incubated for 1 h with compatible secondary antibodies (Anti-rabbit, 1:100, Vector Laboratories, BA-1000). The reaction was revealed with DAB (Sigma-Aldrich). Images were obtained using an Olympus BX60 microscope.

For the biodistribution studies, 3.7 MBq/0.03 mL of GQDs labeled with 99mTc was injected by the retro-orbital route for evaluating the systemic behavior in two groups: group 1 consisted of healthy animals (n = 6), and group 2 animals with endometriosis (n = 6). Briefly, the animals were anesthetized intramuscularly with a mixture of 10% Ketamine and 2% Xylazine. Then, 99mTc-GQDs were administered by retro-orbital injection. Two hours post-injection, both groups were euthanized by asphyxiation (CO2chamber), and the organs of interest (the heart, brain, stomach, intestine, bladder, kidney, lung, liver, spleen, blood, and affected endometrium) were immediately dissected out and weighted for quantitative estimation of gamma counts using a gamma counter (Perkin Elmer, Wizard® 2470). The results were expressed as percentage of injected dose per organ (%ID/g).

The traits of Salmonella enteric serovar Typhimurium standard strains TA98, TA100, and TA102 were phenotypically checked immediately before the use in the experiments. The test tube contained a mixture of 100 μL of one of the eight concentrations of GQDS, 500 μL sodium phosphate buffer (27.6 g/L NaH2PO4∙H2O and 28.4 g/L Na2HPO4; 0.2 M, pH 7.4) and 100 μL of the bacterial suspension (2 × 109 cells/mL). After a pre-incubation for 20 min with stirring, at 37 °C, 2 mL of top agar (7 g/L agar; 5 g/L NaCl; 0.0105 g/L L-histidine; 0.0122 g/L biotin; pH 7.4, 45 °C) was added to the test tube, and the final mixture was poured onto a Petri dish with minimal agar (15 g/L agar, Vogel-Bonner E medium 10× (10 g/L MgSO4∙7H2O; 100 g/L C6H8O7∙H2O; 500 g/L K2HPO4; 175 g/L Na(NH4)HPO4∙4H2O) containing 20 g/L glucose. This final mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 72 h, and His
+ revertant colonies were counted. The sample was considered positive for mutagenicity when a significant response to the analysis of variance (ANOVA) (p ≤ 0.05) and reproducible positive dose-response curve were found. All the experiments were done in triplicate and repeated at least twice [19,20].

The statistical analyses were performed using the GraphPad Prism 6.0 software (GraphPad Software, Inc.). Results are shown as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). To determine statistically significant differences between groups, normal distribution was assumed and one-way ANOVA was used. The differences were considered statistically significant at p < 0.05.

There are several ways to produce GQDs. These methods are divided into two categories: i) top-down strategy and ii) bottom-up strategy [21]. Among these methods can be highlighted: electron beam lithography, hydrothermal route, acidic exfoliation, chemical oxidation (oxidative cleavage), chemical ablation, Laser ablation method, carbonization of organic precursors, solution chemistry, solvothermal synthesis, ultrasonic and irradiation method [21,22]. The electron-beam lithography, laser ablation, ultrasonic method and chemical ablation are limited by the requirement for special equipment, extremely expensive raw materials and low yield. Also the acid exfoliation, chemical oxidation and solvothermal synthesis requires high amounts of chemical solvents as H2SO4 and HNO3 (sulfuric acid and nitric acid respectively). The hydrothermal or electrochemical strategies can be used, but these methods are limited to GO as starting material, and also require a series of chemical treatments which typically take several days and involve lots of chemical reagents. Nevertheless, these methods offered the GQDs with only one emission color (blue or green luminescence) and with limited size [22–24]. A green and environmentally friend route was described by Bayat and Iranizad [25]. In this methodology a low-cost and high-yield green-photoluminescent single-layer GQDs were synthesized with only deionized water and glucose as a precursor. But the size variation was limited and not adequate for biological application [26,27]. Thus, in comparison with all the other methods, including a green route, the electrochemistry methodology described in this study, was capable to produce organic solvent-free and size tailoring GQDs that are well-designed for biological application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size determination by dynamic light scattering
ce:section.ce:para: The DLS (Fig. 1 ) uses the Stokes-Einstein equation, which presumes a spherical shape for the nanoparticles in order to deduce the particle radius and then calculate the translational diffusion constant of the particles. However, GQDS have no rotational symmetry; hence, the DLS analysis would yield two translational diffusion coefficients and also the rotational diffusion coefficient [28,29]. Therefore, assuming that the diameters of our GQDs were larger than the thickness of the 2D flakes, the result of the DLS is near the real size. In this case, and assuming these inferences, our GQDs showed a size of 167.3 nm. The PDI value of 0.507 is not relevant, considering the epigraph, because graphene is a 2D structure with no rotational symmetry.

The Raman spectra revealed the presence of bands at ~1360 cm-1 and ~1600 cm-1 (Fig. 2 ), which are the D and G characteristic bands of graphene, associated with disorder or defects in the lattice and CC vibrations, respectively. Although the samples had been washed with Milli-Q water to minimize the influence of citrate on Raman spectroscopy measurements, bands attributed to the substrate on which the sample was deposited, or remnants of the quantum dots preparation route, such as citrate, can also be observed.

The atomic force microscopy (AFM) results are showed in Fig. 3 . According to the data analysis, the average diameter of the GQDs ranged in the interval of 160–280 nm, while the most frequent heights were about 5 nm. The roughness was very low, with an Rq of 4.58 nm and Ra of 3.42 nm.
We decided to keep the citrate in the sample considering its antioxidant properties that would be relevant for a pharmaceutical/biological application of these GQDs [30].
The optical properties of GQDs are showing in Fig. 5 .
The results showed a maximum absorbance at 265 nm, and the fluorescence emission was observed in the region between 500 and 550 nm, corroborating the photoluminescence properties [31,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Radiochemical purity of the GQDs nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: A simple, rapid, efficient and direct method for labeling GQDs with 99mTc was developed. The labeling efficiency of the 99mTc-GQDs nanoparticles was assessed by RTLC. In RTLC, using acetone as the solvent, free 99mTc moved with the solvent front, while 99mTc-GQDs nanoparticles remained at the spotting point. The radiochemical purity was found to be over 97% for as long as 4 h without any significant changes during the incubation (p > 0.05) (Table 2 ).

The stability of GQDs with 99mTc for4 hours and the high yielding percentage proved that the labeling methodology was efficient and safe, and a similar methodology could be used in order to introduce other radioisotopes for therapeutic purposes too.

The results of the GODs biodistribution in healthy animals are showed in Fig. 6 .Is possible to observe that the 99mTc-GQDs were uptake by liver (23.14%) and spleen (0.61%). Considering that nanoparticles are easily recognized by the mononuclear phagocytic system (MPS) represented mainly by the liver and spleen [33], this result is not surprising. However, the amount of GQDs uptake was very small, which suggests an alternative pathway of nanoparticles avoiding the MPS. The amounts taken up by the small intestine (29.35%) and large intestine (5.85%) apparently confirm enterohepatic excretion of 99mTc-GQDs nanoparticles taken up by liver.

However, the uptake by the stomach (8.25%) needs also be considered. The presence of GQDs in the stomach, considering that retro-orbital injection was used, may be explained by assuming that some of the 99mTc-GQD reach a colloidal state, as observed with 99mTc-Sn(OH)n colloid, and, for that reason, are taken up by the stomach [34]. The data on the kidney excretion are important. Both kidneys (∑ 24.97%) uptake 99mTc-GQDs, and the result of clearance is confirmed by the presence of 99mTc-GQD nanoparticles in the bladder (4.27%), which agrees with the data of Kurantowicz et al. [35]. Although Kurantowicz et al. [35] had studied smaller GO (8-25 nm) is well known that glomerular capillary pores, known as fenestrae are 60–100 nm wide. Nevertheless, biophysical studies demonstrate that fenestrated and nonfenestrated capillaries have comparable permeabilities to macromolecules [36,39]. The very low uptake in the brain corroborates the safety aspect regarding the blood-brain barrier (BBB). In addition, the low blood content of GQDs indicates poor binding with blood proteins, such as albumin. This may be explained by the fact that GQDs nanoparticles have a 2D geometry [38]. The 2D geometry may lead to an erratic spatial conformation, which causes an allosteric impediment and, consequently, difficulty in binding with blood proteins.

The angiogenesis model using the endometriosis induction was confirmed by histology and macro-analysis of the animals (Fig. 7 ). In all animals, endometriosis was confirmed. In addition, the VEGF immunoreactivity observed as the expression of mRNA encoding VEGF was tested, and the results corroborated the high level of VEGF in inducted animals (Fig. 7).

In order to evaluate the possible interaction between the GQDs and the neoangiogenesis environment closely associated with the tumor growth, we evaluated the 99mTc-GQDs biodistribution in the angiogenesis model (endometriosis induction). The results are shown in Fig. 8 .

The results showed that the presence of VEGF and a whole new vascularization zone changed the biological behavior of the 99mTc-GQD. Endometriosis increased the renal clearance (47,78% and 41,51% left and right kidney respectively). Although no information regarding the correlation between endometriosis and augmented renal clearance has been found, which could justify these data, we found that some clinical conditions, such as inflammation, and severe postsurgical conditions in rats, can increase the glomerular filtration rate (GFR) and the renal clearance itself [37]. The uptake in the stomach (5,78%) was almost half of that in healthy animals. The reduced uptake in the intestine (2,47% and 3,31% small and large intestine respectively) may have been correlated with the low uptake in the liver, which confirms the enterohepatic elimination observed in the healthy animals. No information was found on correlation between the liver function and endometriosis. On the other hand, itis known that a healthy liver is also a site of complex immunological activity mediated by a diverse immune cell repertoire, as well as non-hematopoietic cell populations. In a non-diseased liver, metabolic and tissue remodeling functions require elements of inflammation [38]. Therefore, we believe that endometriosis associated with the entire health condition of the body could be responsible for reducing the liver function, and for the lower uptake in the liver observed in endometriotic rats. The uptake in the endometriotic lesion is an important finding. A negligible amount of 99mTc-GQDs was found in the endometriotic lesion, which corroborates that the whole angiogenesis process with a whole new vascularization area did not interfere with the accumulation of 99mTc-GQDs in this zone. This data corroborates the use of GQDs nanoparticles with especial attention for tumor imaging and/or drug delivery template, as stated by Ren et al. [40]. The negligible results confirm that the use of the type of nanoparticles may be tailored and functionalized for this kind of purpose.

Finally is important to notice that in both cases (healthy and inducted, such as male and female animals) no aberrant tissue deposition has been noticed, which corroborates the use aspect related to GQDs as also confirmed by Zhang et al. [41].

According to the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD), all products intended for human use should be evaluated regarding toxicological aspects. It is recommended that at least two in vitro toxicological tests be carried out before proceeding to in vivo experimentation [42]. In general, the preliminary toxicity assessment using the Ames test, which detects point mutations in the DNA sequence, is recommended for any chemical substance used for human or animal health. In the present study, the Salmonella/microsome assay was used to screen the GQDs nanoparticles mutagenicity, following recommendations of the OECD, guideline 471, using the TA98, TA100, TA102, and TA104strains, in the absence of metabolic activation (Fig. 9 ).

As shown here, no frame shift mutagenic activity (TA98 strain) or mutagenicity causing G:C to A:T substitution (TA100) was observed. It was possible to observe the cytotoxic profiles of the GQDs for those strains, mainly at higher concentrations. On the other hand, the strains which detect A:T to G:C substitution (TA102 and TA104) displayed a clear dose-response profile, suggesting that GQDs induced mutations. This pair of strains can detect oxidative DNA damage, and they differ from each other in the proficiency of nucleotide excision repair (NER), where TA102 expresses the uvrB gene, while TA104 does not express it [43]. In this sense, many studies have shown that oxidative stress is one of the mechanisms involved in the toxic effects of the carbon nanomaterial [44]. We observed a discrete increase inTA102 His
+ revertants compared to TA104 at a dose of 0.1 μg, which suggests that, after a DNA lesion, NER repair mechanisms can favor translesion DNA repair [45], resulting in point-mutated sequences and enhancing the GQDs mutagenicity, corroborating the literature information.

In general the results sustained the use of GQDs nanoparticles for human use. The results have shown that, despite some interference with the DNA function, especially related to the oxidative stress, the GQDs nanoparticles seem to be safe. A distinct behavior has been noticed in both models studied, with an augmented renal clearance in animals with endometriosis, which has been related to the increased glomerular filtration rate under this condition. Also has been observed a reduced uptake by the stomach and a very limited uptake by the mononuclear phagocytic system. Under normal conditions (healthy animals) a ubiquitous biodistribution has been observed. The main information relies in the negligible tissue deposition in brain and a possible enterohepatic pathway. The presence of oxygen functional groups imparts high water solubility, which is quite desirable for biomedical application. Finally, the synthetic way to obtain the GQD is an important factor. The classical Hummer's method and its derivations use, for example, hazardous reagents such as oxidant salts and acids making these routes unfavorable for producing GQDs for biomedical application. In this study we used a green route to produce a well-stable GQDs that could be used in animals and which use could translated to humans.
The authors state that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank CAPES (Brazil) and CNPq (Brazil) for the financial support of this research. Also, the Russian Science Foundation, contract number 17-15-01533. I.N. acknowledges support of the Agence Nationale de Recherche through the ERA-NET project ICENAP.
